1980s.title: Price reforms and inflation anxiety
1980s.body: China's market reforms loosened planned prices, and the late-1980s inflation surge made consumer prices a major political and household concern.
1980s.event: Dual-track price reform and 1988 inflation spike
1980s.driver: Price liberalization, credit growth, food prices
1980s.outcome: The route starts in 1986, just as reform-era price pressure became visible.
1980s.evidence: World Bank / IMF IFS annual CPI

1990s.title: Market reforms and mid-decade inflation
1990s.body: Deng Xiaoping's southern tour, investment acceleration, and tax and exchange-rate reforms reshaped China's price system before later stabilization.
1990s.driver: Credit, investment, exchange-rate reform, food prices
1990s.outcome: Inflation rose sharply mid-decade before policy tightening cooled it.

2000s.title: WTO era and global commodity exposure
2000s.body: WTO entry, export manufacturing, urbanization, and global commodity cycles changed how prices moved through China's economy.
2000s.outcome: China became more globally connected, but food and energy still drove household inflation episodes.
2000s.evidence: World Bank / IMF IFS annual CPI

2010s.title: Property cycle and rebalancing
2010s.body: The economy shifted toward services and consumption while property, pork prices, and local-government investment shaped the lived cost environment.
2010s.event: Rebalancing, property expansion, and pork-price cycles
2010s.outcome: Headline CPI stayed moderate in many years, but household costs varied by city and basket.

2020s.title: Pandemic controls and weak demand
2020s.body: COVID controls, reopening, property stress, and softer demand made China's early-2020s price story different from many high-inflation economies.
2020s.event: Zero-COVID period, reopening, and property slowdown
2020s.driver: Demand weakness, food cycles, property stress
2020s.outcome: The 2024 endpoint shows less cumulative CPI pressure than many peers, despite major economic disruption.
